title: Perplexity & Evaluation
learning_outcomes: Derive Perplexity equations from entropy metrics.
Calculate Word Error Rate (WER) using Levenshtein distance matrix alignments.

content_sections.title: Perplexity (PPL) Mathematics
content_sections.content:
Perplexity is a metric for evaluating language models. It measures how well a probability model predicts a test sequence. Mathematically, it is the exponentiated cross-entropy of the model on the sequence.

Given a word sequence $W = (w_1, \dots, w_N)$, the perplexity is:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, \dots, w_N)^{-\frac{1}{N}} = \sqrt[N]{\prod_{i=1}^N \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln \text{PPL}(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \ln P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

This is the definition of cross-entropy. Thus, $\text{PPL}(W) = e^{H(W)}$. A lower perplexity indicates the model is more confident in its predictions.

content_sections.title: Word Error Rate (WER) and Alignment
content_sections.content:
For sequence generation tasks (like speech-to-text or machine translation), we evaluate outputs using **Word Error Rate (WER)**. WER is computed by aligning the hypothesis sequence against a reference sequence using **Levenshtein Distance**:

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N}$$

where:
*   $S$ is the number of substitutions.
*   $D$ is the number of deletions.
*   $I$ is the number of insertions.
*   $N$ is the number of words in the reference sequence.

This alignment is solved using dynamic programming to find the path that minimizes edit distance.
